─[ Unicode Escape Edit Mismatch ]─ ┐

name: unicode-escape-edit-mismatch description: Read files before editing — unicode escapes like \u2014 don’t match literal characters in Edit tool scope: internal user-invocable: false allowed-tools: Read, Edit

Unicode Escape Edit Mismatch

The Edit tool requires exact string matching. When a source file contains unicode escape sequences, pasting the rendered literal character will fail to match.

The Problem

A file contains an escape sequence like \u2014 (em dash). You write an Edit call using the literal character — in old_string. The Edit tool can’t find the string because the bytes don’t match — \u2014 (six ASCII characters) is not the same as — (a three-byte UTF-8 character).

# Source file contains:
title = "Hello \u2014 World"

# This Edit call FAILS — literal em dash doesn't match the escape:
old_string: title = "Hello — World"

# This Edit call SUCCEEDS — exact escape sequence matches:
old_string: title = "Hello \u2014 World"

The Fix

Always Read the file first and copy the exact encoding used in the source. Never assume a unicode character is stored as its literal glyph — it may be an escape sequence (\u2014, \u00e9, \u2019, etc.).

Snags

  • This applies to any escaped representation: \u escapes, \x hex escapes, HTML entities (&mdash;), etc.
  • JSON files almost always use \u escapes for non-ASCII characters
  • The mismatch is invisible in rendered output — both forms look identical when displayed
  • Multiple encoding layers can stack (e.g., a JSON string inside a YAML file)
  • The same character can have different escape forms (\u00e9 vs \xe9 vs é) — match whichever the file actually uses
  • When in doubt, Read the file and look at the raw line before constructing the Edit call
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
